I said, "I want to drink from that old well over there." And I went over to get a drink of water and I pumped it and drink. I leaned across the fence like this, was looking. The baby and wife were picking some violets there in the yard. I begin to think of different things and remember seeing all of us little old boys lined up there: time of the First World War. And they'd…
I was a little old boy and we'd have our hands leaning on their shoulders…?… them stockings down, what had stockings, and--and or shoes, and the toes all out of them, standing there. I remember a time; it's right after Christmas. Mama popped some corn. And we taken some in a sack to school. That was a rare. She popped it to string on the Christmas tree, what she had left over. A little old cedar tree, it was cut down out in the field, put the popcorn around it, get some papers, and make them loops, you know, put it around we got at school.
text_no:
Se, den broren er i herlighet i dag. Å, hvor jeg skulle ønske han var her. Jeg--jeg forteller deg… Ikke for lenge siden kom jeg fra et møte i Texas. Åh, jeg var så sliten. Jeg sa: "La oss kjøre oppover veien." Og vi kjørte oppover veien og passerte den gamle skolen hvor vi pleide å gå. Jeg så på stedet og tenkte: "Åh, min…" Jeg stoppet. Jeg sa: "Jeg vil drikke fra den gamle brønnen der borte." Jeg gikk bort for å få en slurk vann, pumpet og drakk. Jeg lente meg over gjerdet slik som dette, og så meg rundt. Babyen og min kone plukket noen fioler der i hagen. Jeg begynte å tenke på forskjellige ting og husket hvordan vi alle små gutter stod på rekke der: under Første verdenskrig. 
Jeg var en liten gutt og vi hadde hendene hvilende på skuldrene deres... med sokkene nede, for de som hadde sokker, og--og skoene var slitte med tærne som stakk ut, stående der. Jeg husker en gang, det var rett etter jul. Mamma poppet litt mais. Og vi tok noen i en pose med til skolen. Det var en sjeldenhet. Hun poppet dem for å pynte juletreet med de restene vi hadde. Et lite gammelt sedertre, som var hugget ned ute på åkeren, fikk popcorn rundt seg. Vi klippet noen papirløkker, du vet, og satte dem rundt treet på skolen.

text:
And I remember setting there, looking. I remember mother give us that sack of popcorn, and we put it in the cloak room where we used to have to keep our--our coats or our lunches. And I happened to think about that popcorn. I raised up my hand. Teacher said, "What do you want, William?"
I said, "May I be excused?"
Said, "Yes."
I went out to the cloak room, and reached down in there, and got me a great big hand of that popcorn, went out and stood out behind the schoolhouse, and eat that popcorn, and come back in. When dinner time come, we went in to get our lunch and went over the hill. And Edward said to me, he said, "Say, brother, something's happened to that popcorn."
"It sure has, hasn't it?" I knowed what had happened to it. I'd eat it.
text_no:
Jeg husker at jeg satt der og så. Jeg husker at mor ga oss en pose med popcorn, og vi la den i garderoben hvor vi pleide å oppbevare jakkene eller lunsjene våre. Jeg kom til å tenke på popcornet. Jeg rakte opp hånden. Læreren spurte: "Hva vil du, William?"
Jeg sa: "Kan jeg få gå ut?"
Hun svarte: "Ja."
Jeg gikk ut i garderoben, tok en stor håndfull popcorn, gikk ut og stilte meg bak skolebygningen og spiste popcornet. Så kom jeg inn igjen. Da det ble lunsjtid, gikk vi inn for å hente maten vår og dro over åsen. Edward sa til meg: "Hør, bror, det er skjedd noe med popcornet."
"Det har det virkelig," svarte jeg. Jeg visste hva som hadde skjedd med det. Jeg hadde spist det.

text:
Standing there leaning over the fence and thinking about that, I said, "God, I'd give all the world and my life now if I could take him that handful of popcorn to where he was at and give it back to him. He died before we ever come to a place where we'd have very much, just enough to barely live on. God called his precious life.
I was out west working on a cattle ranch, and he died in the hospital calling for me. You could hear his screams plumb down the street, a hollering, "Bill, come to me." One of these days when I cross the portal, I'll be there. That's right. He got saved just before going.
text_no:
Mens jeg sto der og lente meg mot gjerdet og tenkte på det, sa jeg: "Gud, jeg ville gi hele verden og mitt liv nå hvis jeg kunne gi ham tilbake den håndfullen med popcorn. Han døde før vi noensinne fikk særlig mye, bare nok til så vidt å klare oss. Gud kalte hans dyrebare liv.
Jeg var ute vestpå og jobbet på en kvegfarm, og han døde på sykehuset mens han ropte etter meg. Du kunne høre hans skrik hele veien nedover gaten, ropende: 'Bill, kom til meg.' En dag, når jeg krysser portalen, vil jeg være der. Det er sant. Han ble frelst rett før han gikk bort."

text: I remember looking at it there and thinking about that. I thought… I looked up on the hill where we used to sleigh ride. I remember it about 1930--1917. Mama worked for the government, sewed shirts for the soldiers. They had bundles of shirts, and that's how we lived. They have to go down of a weekend, take the shirts down. She got four dollars and forty-four cents to make a bundle of shirts. That's what we lived on.
text_no: Jeg husker hvordan jeg så på det og tenkte på det. Jeg så opp på bakken der vi pleide å kjøre akebrett. Jeg husker det fra rundt 1930--1917. Mamma arbeidet for regjeringen og sydde skjorter til soldatene. De hadde bunker med skjorter, og slik levde vi. Hun måtte gå ned i løpet av en helg for å levere skjortene. Hun fikk fire dollar og førtifire cent for å lage en bunke skjorter. Det var det vi levde av.

text: All right. When she was going down, I remember us little boys… She couldn't buy us a sled. And all the boys had sleds out on the hill riding. Come a great snow and sleeted and froze over the top, and we would slide down the hills on our sled. Brother and I didn't have any sled, so we went out to the old country dump, got a big old dish pan. And we'd set in the dish pan and put our legs around one another and hugged each other. Down the hill we'd go. We might not have been as much class as the rest of them, but we were riding just the same, so we… That done all right until the bottom came out of the thing; then we couldn't ride no more in that. So we went down and got us an old log. We'd get up on the hill and get on this log, and here we would go down the hill on the log. Oh, my.
text_no: Da hun holdt på å gå nedover, husker jeg at vi små gutter… Hun hadde ikke råd til å kjøpe oss en kjelke. Alle guttene ute på bakken hadde kjelker og lekte. Det kom et stort snøfall, og etterpå frøs det til is på toppen. Vi akte ned bakkene på kjelkene våre. Bror og jeg hadde ingen kjelke, så vi dro til den gamle søppelfyllingen i bygda og fant en stor, gammel kjel. Vi satte oss i kjelen, omslynget hverandres ben og holdt fast. Nedover bakken bar det. Vi var kanskje ikke like stilfulle som de andre, men vi akte like fullt. Dette fungerte bra helt til bunnen falt ut av kjelen; da kunne vi ikke bruke den lenger. Så fant vi en gammel stokk. Vi klatret opp bakken, satte oss på stokken, og akte ned igjen. Å, du verden.

text:
I remember during the time I wanted to be a soldier. I'd see those soldiers come up from Utica Pike, that flag a flying, my, the banner is rolling, the drums a beating. I'd stand there, a little bitty boy, and my mouth open, my hair hanging down in my face. Oh, when I get to be a man, I'm going to be a soldier. I'd seen with them leggings on and standing. They'd holler, "Attention," and everybody'd stand…?… I'd say, "Oh, my." And when I got old enough and the war come on, they wouldn't receive me. One thing, being a minister, and another thing, I guess I just wasn't man enough to go. They wouldn't take me. I tried many times, tried to volunteer, and they said, "We'll call you if we need you. Reverend Branham, go on back."
I thought, "Well…" But friends, I finally got to put on a uniform. That's right.
text_no:
Jeg husker tiden da jeg ønsket å bli soldat. Jeg så soldatene komme opp fra Utica Pike med flagget vaiende, banneret rullende og trommene slående. Jeg sto der som en liten gutt, med munnen åpen og håret hengende ned i ansiktet. "Å, når jeg blir voksen, skal jeg bli soldat," tenkte jeg ofte. Jeg så dem med leggingsene og stående i posisjon. De ropte "Tiltred!" og alle sto i giv akt. Jeg sa til meg selv, "Å, wow."
Da jeg endelig ble gammel nok, og krigen kom, ville de ikke ta meg inn. Én grunn var at jeg var forkynner, og en annen var kanskje at jeg ikke var mann nok. De avviste meg. Jeg prøvde mange ganger å verve meg, men de sa alltid, "Vi kaller deg hvis vi trenger deg. Reverend Branham, gå tilbake."
Jeg tenkte, "Ja vel..." Men kjære venner, jeg fikk endelig på meg en uniform. Det stemmer.

text:
I remember Lloyd Ford, a friend of mine. He used to ro--belong to what they call the Lone Scouts. They sold papers called "Path Finder" or something. And he got a Boy Scout suit for that. Now, I… Oh, I admired that boy. I said to him, I said, "Lloyd, when you wear that out will you give it to me?"
He said, "Sure, I'll give it to you." But my, that suit lasted a long time, looked like it. It got to a place till I thought the thing never would wear out. So finally he said he'd give it to me when it wore out. Well, I went on for a while. After while I missed him wearing it. I said, "Lloyd, what become of the suit."
He said, "Well, Billy, I'll see if I can find it." He looked around, and come back, and said, "Well, Billy," he said, "the--the last piece of it," said, "just went to pieces, and--and mother taken the coat and made the dog a pallet." And said, "It's all ruined and gone now." Said, "I couldn't find a piece of it, only just one legging."
I said, "Well, bring that to me." Just one legging, a little--had a drawstring, little legging about like that. And he brought that one legging to me, and I thought, "Oh, my, how I like that legging." So I put it on at home, you know, and I thought, "How dandy. If I can just let the school kids see me with that legging on…"
text_no:
Jeg husker Lloyd Ford, en venn av meg. Han pleide å tilhøre det de kalte Lone Scouts. De solgte aviser som het "Path Finder" eller noe lignende, og han fikk en speideruniform for det. Jeg beundret den gutten. Jeg sa til ham: "Lloyd, når du har slitt ut uniformen, kan du gi den til meg?"
Han svarte: "Selvfølgelig, jeg skal gi den til deg." Men den uniformen varte lenge, så ut som den aldri skulle bli slitt ut. Til slutt sa han at han skulle gi den til meg når den var ubrukelig. Tiden gikk, og en dag la jeg merke til at han ikke lenger hadde på seg uniformen. Jeg spurte: "Lloyd, hva skjedde med uniformen?"
Han svarte: "Vel, Billy, jeg skal se om jeg finner den." Han lette rundt og kom tilbake og sa: "Vel, Billy, den siste delen av den gikk i oppløsning, og mor brukte jakken til å lage en liggeplass til hunden." Og så sa han: "Den er helt ødelagt og borte nå. Jeg kunne ikke finne noe annet enn en enkelt legg."
Jeg sa: "Vel, ta med den til meg." Det var bare en legg, med en snor, omtrent sånn. Han tok med leggen til meg, og jeg tenkte: "Å, så glad jeg er for denne leggen." Jeg tok den på hjemme og tenkte: "Så flott. Hvis jeg bare kan få skolebarna til å se meg med denne leggen på…"

text:
So I went down that day. I remember going to school. And I got up on the hill just right, and I thought, "Now, how am I going to do? I've got to find excuse to put that legging on so they won't know that that's the only legging I got. So I--I don't know what to do." And I put it back in my coat, and I went on to the school. And there Edward and I were riding down the hill on this old log, and I--I turned over on the log, and I act like I hurt my leg. I said, "Oh, my. " Never hurt. I said, "Oh, my, mmm, whew, that was a strain on my leg." I said, "It just reminds me; I've got one of my leggings…?… in here." I said, "That'll help my leg a whole lot." And I put it on and went into school.
I was going to… You know the old blackboards we used to have at school? I was going to write on the blackboard, so I did this-a-way (See?), put my leg like this one the outside--one that had the legging. I turned sideways like this and write…?… Everybody and--and all the kiddies got to laughing at me, and…?… like that, and they… I got to cry. The teacher made me go home. Had one legging…
text_no:
Jeg gikk dit den dagen. Jeg husker at jeg dro til skolen. Da jeg kom opp på bakken, tenkte jeg: "Hvordan skal jeg gjøre dette? Jeg må finne en unnskyldning for å ta på meg gamasjen, så de ikke skjønner at det er den eneste jeg har." Jeg visste ikke hva jeg skulle gjøre, så jeg la den tilbake i frakken og fortsatte til skolen. Der red Edward og jeg ned bakken på en gammel stokk. Jeg veltet meg over på stokken og lot som jeg hadde skadet beinet. Jeg sa: "Oi, det var vondt." Selv om det ikke gjorde vondt, fortsatte jeg: "Oi, det var en belastning på beinet mitt." Jeg sa: "Det minner meg om at jeg har en av gamasjene mine her. Det vil hjelpe beinet mitt mye." Så tok jeg den på og gikk inn på skolen.
Jeg skulle skrive på tavlen, du vet, den gamle typen vi hadde på skolen. Så jeg gjorde slik (viser), satte beinet med gamasjen på utsiden og snudde meg sidelengs for å skrive. Alle barna begynte å le av meg. Jeg begynte å gråte, og læreren ba meg gå hjem. Jeg hadde bare én gamasje.

text: But brother, sister, today I've got a uniform, and I'm in the army, the army of the Lord, fighting the foes of the wicked, dressed… You might not be able to see it, but I know it's there; I feel it, the armor of God: the full Gospel in my heart; baptism of the Holy Spirit; God working signs and wonders; the helmet of faith--shield of faith; and the helmet of--of salvation; shod with the Gospel; the sword in hand, joined with you, ranks out here now, that our armor's inside of us.
text_no: Men Bror, søster, i dag har jeg en uniform, og jeg er i Herrens hær, kjemper mot de onde krefter, kledd... Du ser det kanskje ikke, men jeg vet den er der; jeg føler den, Guds rustning: det fulle Evangeliet i mitt hjerte; dåpen i Den Hellige Ånd; Gud som virker tegn og under; troens hjelm og skjold; frelsens hjelm; iført Evangeliet; med sverdet i hånden, med Deg, står vi samlet her, med rustningen inni oss.

text:
Looking up there and think of the those old times, I started crying; wife said, "I thought you come home to rest."
I said, "Honey, I think about those things. I think, 'Where is Ralph Fields?'" Said, "Gone." "Where's Wilmer?" Gone. "Where's my brother, Edward?" He's gone. "Where's Rollin Halloway?" Gone. Here we have no continuing city.
I looked back up on the hill to see where the old place was. I thought if I can just visualize seeing that little old line, little old--through that broom sedge there, going up to the house… Gone. The house is gone. Daddy's gone. Oh, my, here we have no continuing city, but we're seeking one to come whose Builder and Maker is God.
My friends are gone; my brother's gone; daddy's gone; my home's gone. Mother and I left, for the story.
text_no:
Da jeg så opp dit og tenkte på de gamle tidene, begynte jeg å gråte. Min kone sa: "Jeg trodde du kom hjem for å hvile." 
Jeg svarte: "Kjære, jeg tenker på disse tingene. Jeg tenker, 'Hvor er Ralph Fields?' Borte. 'Hvor er Wilmer?' Borte. 'Hvor er min bror, Edward?' Han er borte. 'Hvor er Rollin Halloway?' Borte. Her har vi ingen varig by." 
Jeg så opp på åsen for å se hvor det gamle stedet var. Jeg tenkte, hvis jeg bare kan visualisere den lille stien gjennom bråtemarka som fører opp til huset... Borte. Huset er borte. Pappa er borte. Å, min, her har vi ingen varig by, men vi søker en som skal komme, hvor Byggeren og Skaperen er Gud.
Mine venner er borte; min bror er borte; pappa er borte; mitt hjem er borte. Mor og jeg er de eneste som er igjen for å fortelle historien.

text: Notice, then… Think of all those things and how I cheated my brother out of a handful of popcorn. It all come back to me then. Brother, sister, don't never do anything wrong, for it'll come back to you just as sure as you're living. Do right; you're bound to come out right.
text_no: Legg merke til dette… Tenk på alt dette, og hvordan jeg lurte min bror for en håndfull popcorn. Det kom tilbake til meg da. Brødre og søstre, gjør aldri noe galt, for det vil komme tilbake til dere så sikkert som dere lever. Gjør det rette; dere vil garantert komme ut riktig.

text:
And then, I remember… I'll have to hurry 'cause it's getting late, get right down now, it's… Have to leave in a few moments. And you'll give me your attention as you have been, I think you're awfully nice.
On down I wish I had time to move in and get the story of the coming of the gift and so forth, but I'll have to kindly skip that. I remember the time (many of you'll read it in book and so forth), how the Angel of the Lord appeared to me down, told me never to smoke or to drink or defile my body in any way, that there'd be a work for me to do when I got older: appeared to me in that bush, and how they have misunderstood it. My mother could give you the story, standing here. And how that even my best girlfriend, one time, called me a big sissy because that I wouldn't smoke a cigarette.
text_no:
Jeg må skynde meg, for det begynner å bli sent, og jeg må gå om få øyeblikk. Om du gir meg oppmerksomhet som du har gjort til nå, setter jeg stor pris på det.
Jeg skulle ønske jeg hadde tid til å dele hele historien om hvordan gaven kom og alt det andre, men jeg må dessverre hoppe over det. Jeg husker tiden (mange av dere har sikkert lest om det i boken), da Herrens Engel viste seg for meg og ba meg om aldri å røyke, drikke eller besmitte kroppen min på noen måte, fordi det var en oppgave for meg når jeg ble eldre. Han viste seg for meg i en busk, og mange har misforstått det. Min mor kunne fortelle hele historien, stående her. Til og med min beste venninne en gang kalte meg en pyse fordi jeg ikke ville røyke en sigarett.

text:
And I always had my opinion of a cigarette smoking woman and I haven't changed it. That's right. It's the lowest thing that women ever done. That's right. To see them setting in a place… The other day I started to holler, "Fire." right here in this city. A woman setting there, and smoke coming all out around her like that, and a poor little baby laying in her arms; I thought, "You want that baby eyes being ash trays? God never give you that baby for that purpose: your duty to raise it right."
And women, listen here, take my advice, if you do smoke, oh, in Jesus' Name forget about it. Never do that; that's awful. Don't do that for I know if this spirit that bothers me… Now, don't get up and go, everybody will know how guilty you are. See?
Now, and I know one thing, if this Angel of the Lord comes from God, as I know It does, you'll certainly answer at the day of judgment for doing it. We're in the spirit of the latter days: people, heady, high-minded, can't tell them nothing; they know it all. They won't stop and listen to human, to reasoning. Listen. Stop those things. Live right and live before God.
text_no:
Jeg har alltid ment at røykende kvinner oppfører seg uverdig, og det har jeg ikke endret mening om. Det er det laveste kvinner kan gjøre. Her om dagen hadde jeg lyst til å rope "Brann" da jeg så en kvinne sitte med røyk rundt seg, og en stakkars liten baby i armene. Jeg tenkte: "Vil du at babyens øyne skal bli askebegere? Gud ga deg ikke den babyen for at du skulle behandle den slik. Det er din plikt å oppdra barnet på riktig måte."
Kvinner, lytt til meg. Hvis dere røyker, så slutt med det i Jesu Navn. Ikke gjør det; det er forferdelig. Ikke gjør det, for jeg vet at denne ånden som plager meg... Nå, ikke reis dere og gå, for da vil alle vite hvor skyldige dere er.
Jeg vet en ting: Hvis denne Herrens Engel kommer fra Gud, slik jeg tror, vil dere definitivt stå til ansvar på dommens dag for å gjøre dette. Vi er i ånden av de siste dager: mennesker er hovmodige og egenrådige, de tror de vet alt og vil ikke lytte til fornuft. Hør her. Stopp med disse tingene. Lev rett og lev for Gud.

text:
I tell you one thing, you get the Holy Spirit, and then you can go ahead and smoke after you get the Holy Spirit. Someone said to me the other day, "Did you ever baptize anybody that smoked?"
I said, "Look here, brother, I haven't a yardstick at my church. (See?) I teach the people what's the truth, and then when they receive Christ, Christ takes care of the rest of it."
Here's there's an old oak tree out here. It holds its leaves all the year through, all the winter. Springtime come, there's them old dead leaves hanging on it. You don't have to go pick the leaves off to make new ones come on. Just let the new life come up, the old leaves goes off. That's right. Let the Holy Spirit come into a person's heart, they clean up and fix up and a few things like. That's right. Just get them to Christ; that's all. Then it's God's business from then on.
text_no:
Jeg sier deg én ting: Få Den Hellige Ånd, og så kan du røyke etterpå hvis du vil. Noen spurte meg nylig: "Har du noen gang døpt noen som røykte?"
Jeg svarte: "Hør her, bror, vi har ingen målestokk i vår menighet. Jeg lærer folket hva som er sannhet, og når de mottar Kristus, tar Kristus seg av resten."
Ta for eksempel et gammelt eiketre. Det holder på bladene hele vinteren. Når våren kommer, henger de gamle døde bladene fortsatt på. Du trenger ikke plukke dem av for at nye skal vokse. La bare det nye livet komme til, så faller de gamle bladene av. Det samme gjelder med Den Hellige Ånd. Når Han kommer inn i en persons hjerte, renser og fornyer Han. Det er riktig. Før dem til Kristus; det er alt. Deretter er det Guds ansvar.

text: Now, I never tell people what they have to do, and what they can't do, and what… That's up, between them and God, but I know that one of the lowest things that I ever see women do is smoking. I stand here and see them set there and act like that, it's just something in me. I wished I didn't feel that way, but it's something in me makes me feel that way. And I… It's not me; it's Him. And I know by that what it'll be at the judgment; so escape that. You don't have to do it. Get away from it. Stay away from it. You can't come in this prayer line without it being called out on you; that's one thing for sure. He'll call it every time.
text_no: Jeg forteller aldri folk hva de må gjøre eller hva de ikke kan gjøre; det er opp til dem og Gud. Men jeg vet at en av de verste tingene jeg ser kvinner gjøre, er å røyke. Når jeg ser dem sitte der og oppføre seg slik, er det noe i meg som reagerer. Jeg skulle ønske jeg ikke følte det slik, men det er noe i meg som får meg til å føle det. Og det er ikke meg; det er Ham. Jeg vet hva dette innebærer ved dommen, så unngå det. Du trenger ikke gjøre det. Kom deg bort fra det. Hold deg unna. Du kan ikke komme inn i denne bønnekøen uten at det blir påpekt; det er helt sikkert. Han vil påpeke det hver gang.

text:
And notice this one thing now. Later on down through life, I--I had girlfriends like all boys. And I remember I was a little skeptic of girls; I seen the way women act. And frankly, I never did have very much use for women. I don't mean you sisters, now, but I… Just to see how untrue they were, some of them… I was around, watched my father drinking and hung around those places, and maybe I'd be around, and I'd see how women come out and living untrue. And a lot of them women's done gone on to meet the judgment now, and will have to stand there in that day.
And I said I'd never get married; never want nothing to do with one. I'll be a trapper and a hunter all my life, and I'll never have nothing to do with the girl. So when I got to be about… When I was even seventeen, eighteen years old, I'd pass down the street. I'd see a girl on one side, and I thought she was going say something, I'd cross over and go over on the other side, because I just didn't want nothing to do. That's all, didn't want to hook up--caught up with it or anything, I wanted to keep away from it. And so I went ahead. But finally I found a girl that was a real girl. She was--was a Christian. She afterwards become my wife.
text_no:
Legg merke til dette: Senere i livet hadde jeg, som alle gutter, kjærester. Jeg var imidlertid litt skeptisk til jenter fordi jeg hadde sett hvordan noen kvinner oppførte seg. Ærlig talt hadde jeg aldri så mye til overs for kvinner generelt. Misforstå meg ikke, jeg mener ikke dere søstre, men... Noen av dem levde uærlige liv. Jeg så dette fordi jeg var rundt min far som drakk og hang rundt slike steder, og det gjorde meg skeptisk. Mange av disse kvinnene har nå gått bort og må møte dommen.
Jeg sa til meg selv at jeg aldri skulle gifte meg; jeg ville aldri ha noe med kvinner å gjøre. Jeg planla å bli en fellefangstmann og jeger hele mitt liv og holde meg unna jenter. Da jeg var rundt 17-18 år gammel, hvis jeg så en jente på den ene siden av gaten, krysset jeg over til den andre siden for å unngå kontakt. Jeg ønsket rett og slett ikke å bli involvert på noen måte og holdt meg unna dem. Men til slutt møtte jeg en jente som virkelig var annerledes. Hun var en kristen og ble senere min kone.

text:
I guess you wonder how I ever got married being so bashful. I tell you how that happened, just quickly as I can. I met her. She was a--a pretty girl, but she was such a lady, the way she carried herself. And I'm so glad that her little boy, hearing--this afternoon he's hearing this, and I can say his mother was a genuine lady. Yes, sir.
And I met her; she was a member of a church up there. And she asked me to go to church with her, and I did. I kept going with her a long time, and I--I thought she was pretty. She was a real girl, so I… But the only thing was, I'd been going with her for about a year and something, and I knew a girl like that, I couldn't take up of her time if I wasn't going to get married, so I'd have to let somebody else that wanted to get married have her, and that was going to be an awful spot to pull away from me.
text_no:
Du lurer kanskje på hvordan jeg i det hele tatt giftet meg, siden jeg er så sjenert. Jeg skal fortelle deg hvordan det gikk til, så raskt jeg kan. Jeg møtte henne. Hun var en vakker jente, men hun var også en ekte dame i måten hun førte seg på. Jeg er så glad for at hennes lille gutt, som hører dette i ettermiddag, kan vite at hans mor var en genuin dame. Ja, sir.
Jeg møtte henne; hun var medlem av en menighet der oppe. Hun ba meg med i menigheten, og det gjorde jeg. Jeg fortsatte å følge henne i lang tid, og jeg syntes hun var vakker. Hun var en ekte jente, så jeg... Men problemet var at etter å ha vært sammen med henne i over et år, visste jeg at en jente som henne ikke ville ha tid til meg om jeg ikke var villig til å gifte meg. Så jeg måtte la noen andre som ville gifte seg ha henne, og det ville bli svært vanskelig å gi slipp.

text:
So you know how you think they all look, you know, with teeth like pearl, and eyes like a dove, you know, and on like that. All of you get them…
Don't you think that of your wife, you know? You ought to continue thinking that of her too. That's right. That's right. Keep that thought in mind, just as sweet… Wait till she's made immortal on the other side, then look at her. She won't need any--what do you call them manicure stuff you put on your face here to make her look right then. God will have it…
text_no: Som dere alle forestiller dere, med tenner som perler og øyne som en due. Dere ser vel det slik ... gjør dere ikke? Tenker dere ikke slik om deres hustru også? Dere burde fortsette å tenke slik om dem. Det er riktig. Behold den tanken, like søtt... Vent til hun er gjort udødelig på den andre siden, se på henne da. Hun trenger ikke noe - hva kaller dere det? - sminke for å se bra ut da. Gud vil sørge for det...

text:
I believe if a… I can't believe scientists that women, when they was a ugly looking thing, and whatever they was with men with hair out his nose like that and look a prehistoric animal. I believe that when God woke Adam up to look at Eve, she was the most beautiful thing that eyes has ever looked at. That's right. It goes to show that man, even this day, the desire in men, craves--look at a beautiful woman. Why? Because that--that strain comes down through that time (See?) like that, to know that, that that was given plumb from garden of Eden.
And I can see her setting there, her lovely hair hanging down around her hips. Adam looked at her: it was the flesh of his flesh and bone of his bone. And see him take her by arm and walk down through Eden, oh, my. Don't worry, mother, it'll be that way someday again. That's right. There'll be no old there, and there'll be no babies; they'll all be about one age, marching on.
text_no:
Jeg tror ikke på vitenskapen som sier at kvinner en gang så ut som stygge skapninger og at menn hadde hår ut av nesen og så ut som forhistoriske dyr. Jeg tror at da Gud vekket Adam for å se på Eva, var hun det vakreste øynene noen gang hadde sett. Det stemmer. Dette viser at mann, selv i dag, har et dyptliggende ønske om å se på en vakker kvinne. Hvorfor? Fordi denne lengselen stammer fra Edens hage (forstår du?), der skjønnhet ble gitt dem.
Og jeg kan se henne der, med sitt vakre hår hengende ned til hoftene. Adam så på henne: hun var kjøtt av hans kjøtt og ben av hans ben. Se for deg hvordan han tok henne i armen og de gikk sammen gjennom Eden, å, min. Ikke bekymre deg, mor, det vil bli slik igjen en dag. Det stemmer. Det vil ikke finnes alderdom, og det vil ikke finnes babyer; alle vil være omtrent på samme alder og marsjere videre.

text:
Notice, then what happened? I thought, "Well now, I've got to ask her, and I just haven't got the heart to ask her. I don't why. I've got to let her go if she won't marry me.
Now, her father was making about five hundred and something dollars a month, and I was making twenty cents a hour digging ditches. My… I had an old crippled up Ford, was in awful condition. And then I had a… That's about all I had. I didn't even have a suit. I had a trousers of one kind and a coat of another. But she loved me, I know; and I know I loved her.
And I'd go to church with her and she was very sweet girl. She was just peaceful and nice. The I thought, "Now, I've got to do something about it. And what am I going to do?" And I didn't have nerve enough to ask her. Do you know what I done? I wrote her a letter. Sure did. That's an awful way to become married but (See?)--but I would…
Now, it wasn't just, "Dear Miss…" it had a little, you know…?… I fixed it up the best that I could, and I--I got it all fixed out, you know. I took it going to work, and I--I--I dropped it in the mail. Well, when Wednesday night was coming, we had a date to go to church, and I--I begin to think, "What if her mother got ahold of that?" Oh, my.
text_no:
Merk hva som skjedde videre. Jeg tenkte: "Vel, nå må jeg spørre henne, men jeg har bare ikke hjertet til å gjøre det. Jeg vet ikke hvorfor. Jeg må la henne gå hvis hun ikke vil gifte seg med meg."
Hennes far tjente rundt fem hundre dollar i måneden, mens jeg tjente tjue cent i timen ved å grave grøfter. Jeg hadde en gammel, skranglete Ford i elendig tilstand. Det var omtrent alt jeg eide. Jeg hadde ikke engang en hel dress; bare bukser av én type og jakke av en annen. Men hun elsket meg, det visste jeg, og jeg visste at jeg elsket henne.
Jeg dro til menigheten med henne, og hun var en veldig søt jente. Hun var bare fredfull og hyggelig. Så jeg tenkte: "Nå må jeg gjøre noe med det. Hva skal jeg gjøre?" Og jeg hadde ikke nok nerver til å spørre henne personlig. Vet du hva jeg gjorde? Jeg skrev et brev til henne. Det gjorde jeg virkelig. Det er en forferdelig måte å bli gift på, men (forstår du?)...
Brevet var ikke bare, "Kjære frøken...", jeg la litt mer i det. Jeg skrev det så godt jeg kunne. Jeg tok det med meg på vei til jobb, og jeg la det i posten. Vel, da onsdagskvelden kom, hadde vi en avtale om å gå til menigheten, og jeg begynte å tenke: "Hva hvis moren hennes fikk tak i brevet?" Å, min.

text: Now, her father was a good hearted Dutchman and… But her mother is a fine woman, but one of those prissy types, you know, and I… She didn't like me very well. So never mistreated me, that… She just didn't like me. So I thought, "Oh, how am I going to get by her?" So I begin to think when Wednesday night come on, "What if she got that letter and she's the one that meets me on the porch? Now what am I going to do?" I begin to think, "I just won't go." Then I thought, Well, if I don't, I've lost my girlfriend. Now what am I going to do?" I had to do something. So I went on up. I thought, "Oh, I'll take a chance."
text_no: Hennes far var en godhjertet nederlender, men moren hennes var en skikkelig fin dame, om enn litt prippent anlagt. Hun likte meg ikke særlig godt. Hun mishandlet meg aldri, men hun likte meg bare ikke. Jeg tenkte: "Hvordan skal jeg komme meg forbi henne?" Da onsdag kveld nærmet seg, tenkte jeg: "Hva om hun har mottatt brevet og er den som møter meg på verandaen? Hva skal jeg gjøre da?" Jeg overveide å ikke dra, men innså at hvis jeg ikke gjorde det, ville jeg miste kjæresten min. Jeg måtte gjøre noe. Så jeg bestemte meg for å ta sjansen og dro dit.

text:
And I went up there and nobody out. I knowed better than to blow the horn for her to come out. She'd tell me about that if I wasn't man enough to walk up the door and ask for her, I didn't go out. I think that girls that takes that attitude now, would be a lot better off. That's right. That's right.
And so I went up to the door, and I knocked at the door, and getting kind of warm, you know. And I thought, "Who's coming to the door."
And she opened the door, she said, "Oh, hello, Billy."
And I said, "Hello, honey." She looked at me and I thought, "Oh, my, my, my, here it is."
Then she said, "Won't you come in?"
I thought, "Oh, they get me in there, then I know I'm in for it." You know. I said, "I--I--I'll just wait right here on the porch."
"…?… step in." Said, "Mother's here."
And I thought, "I know not to step in." So…
Said, "Mother…?… Step in."
I said, "Aw, well, now. Is it all right if I wait right…"
And she said, "No, step in."
text_no:
Jeg gikk dit opp, og det var ingen å se. Jeg visste bedre enn å tute med hornet for å få henne ut. Hun hadde gitt meg beskjed om at hvis jeg ikke var mann nok til å gå til døren og spørre etter henne, så kunne jeg bare la være å komme. Jeg tror at jenter som har den holdningen i dag, ville hatt det mye bedre. Det stemmer. 
Så jeg gikk til døren og banket på. Det begynte å bli litt varmt, vet du. Jeg tenkte: "Hvem kommer til døren nå?"
Hun åpnet døren og sa: "Å, hei, Billy."
"Hei, kjære," svarte jeg. Hun så på meg, og jeg tenkte: "Å herregud, nå skjer det."
Så spurte hun: "Vil du komme inn?"
Jeg tenkte: "Hvis jeg går inn der, vet jeg at jeg er ferdig." Så jeg sa: "Jeg— jeg— jeg venter her på verandaen."
"Kom inn, mor er her," insisterte hun.
Jeg visste at jeg ikke skulle gå inn, men hun gjentok: "Mor er her, kom inn." 
Jeg svarte: "Er det greit om jeg venter her ute?"
Hun sa bestemt: "Nei, kom inn."

text:
So I stepped and just stood, and just barely stepped in too. She shut the door, and I waited a little while and I thought, "Oh, my." She got ready to come out. I thought, "Well, must be all right." So I thought, "What's she going to say now? This is my last day. I know that. Boy, she's going to tell me, twenty cents a hour, I could never make her a living like that, living in a home that she does. So I went on, went down to church that night, and I was thinking, "Oh, my." right after church, had it made up to…
See there, you cross the bridges before you get to it? I said, "Now, well she'll tell me after church, 'Well, Billy, I'm sorry but is our last night together.'" I could just hear her say it. Oh, I just knowed she was going to say it. So I went out. I never heard a thing that Dr. Davis said, and he just preached on. I never heard a thing. I--I was worrying about what was going to happen when I got outside the church.
text_no:
Så jeg gikk frem og sto der, knapt nok innenfor døren. Hun stengte døren, og jeg ventet litt mens jeg tenkte, "Å, kjære." Hun gjorde seg klar til å komme ut. Jeg tenkte, "Vel, det må være greit." Så tenkte jeg, "Hva kommer hun til å si nå? Dette er min siste dag. Jeg vet det. Hun vil sikkert si at tjue cent i timen aldri kan forsørge henne, spesielt i et hjem som hennes." Så jeg gikk videre og dro til menigheten den kvelden, og jeg tenkte, "Å, kjære." Rett etter møtet hadde jeg allerede forestilt meg hvordan det ville gå...
Ser du, man bekymrer seg ofte unødvendig på forhånd? Jeg sa til meg selv, "Nå, hun vil nok si etter møtet, 'Vel, Billy, jeg er lei for det, men dette er vår siste kveld sammen.'" Jeg kunne nesten høre henne si det. Jeg visste bare at hun kom til å si det. Så jeg gikk ut. Jeg fikk ikke med meg noe av hva Dr. Davis sa, han forkynte bare videre. Jeg hørte ingenting. Jeg var opptatt med å bekymre meg for hva hun ville si etter at jeg kom ut av menigheten.

text:
That night when we left the church and we started walking home, you know, I was walking down, and the moon was shining bright. She said, "Did you work hard, Billy?"
I said, "I sure did." I thought, "She never got that letter. (See?) She never got that letter. It hung up in the mail box, or it hasn't been delivered." I got pretty brave then. And I said, "All right." I begin to look over at her, look her right in the face. And I thought she passed through, and that moon shine on her, how pretty she looked. You know? So I thought, "Oh, my, whew, I hope she don't get that letter now 'cause I…" going on like that, and I was getting pretty brave, you know.
She said, "Billy."
And I said, "Yes."
She said, "I got your letter."
Thought, "Oh…" I said, "You did?"
She said, "Uh-huh." Just kept on walking…
I thought, "Well, woman, say something. Tell me…?… something." You know how women can keep you in suspense. You brothers know what I'm talking about. And I'm… I just kept walking on, so I… I said, "Well, maybe she never read it." I said, "Did you read it?"
She said, "Uh-huh." She kept walking on. I thought, "Oh, my, say something 'fore you get up there at the house, sure enough." So…
And I said, "Did you read all of it?"
She said, "Uh-huh."
I said, "What'd you think about it?" you know.
Said, "It was all right."
text_no:
Den kvelden da vi forlot menigheten og begynte å gå hjem, var månen lysende klar. Hun sa, "Jobbet du hardt, Billy?"
Jeg svarte, "Det gjorde jeg." Jeg tenkte, "Hun fikk aldri det brevet. Det må ha blitt hengende i postkassen eller ikke blitt levert." Jeg følte meg ganske modig da. Jeg sa, "Greit." Jeg begynte å se på henne, rett i ansiktet. Måneskinnet falt på henne, og hun så så vakker ut. Du vet, jeg tenkte, "Å, Gud, jeg håper hun ikke får det brevet nå…" Jeg ble stadig modigere.
Hun sa, "Billy."
Jeg sa, "Ja."
Hun svarte, "Jeg fikk brevet ditt."
Jeg tenkte, "Å…" Jeg sa, "Du gjorde det?"
Hun nikket og fortsatte å gå.
Jeg tenkte, "Vel, kvinne, si noe. Fortell meg noe." Du vet hvordan kvinner kan holde deg i spenning. Brødre, dere vet hva jeg snakker om. Jeg fortsatte å gå, og så sa jeg, "Kanskje hun aldri leste det." Jeg spurte, "Leste du det?"
Hun nikket. Jeg tenkte, "Å, Gud, si noe før vi kommer hjem." 
Jeg spurte, "Leste du alt?"
Hun svarte, "Ja."
Jeg spurte, "Hva syntes du om det?"
Hun svarte, "Det var greit."

text:
Well, we got married. There you are, so she…?… We got married. And just before we got married I knowed I had to--to ask her parents for her. She said, "You'll have to ask mother and daddy."
I thought, "Oh, oh. I'm in for it again." I said, "How about just asking your daddy?"
She said, "Oh, either one of them."
I said, "Thank you, honey." I went out and remember that night, I was going to ask her daddy and I set down so nervous. He lightened at… So I said… I was so nervous, and she'd look over at me, laughing. And then so I got up off the--the duofold. I walked over, and she said, "Good night."
And I said, "Good night." I said, "A-hem, Mr. Brumbach…"
He said, "Yeah…"
I said, "Could I--I--I speak to you just a moment?"
He said, "Sure."
He come walking out, you know, great big fellow, walked out on the porch, and I said, "Sure is a fine night."
He said, "Yes, it is, Billy."
I said, "My, I like this weather, don't you?" And I'm so nervous I didn't know what to do.
He said, "Yes." Said, "Go ahead, Bill, you can have her."
I said, "Do you mean it, Charlie?"
He said, "Yeah."
I like him today. My, I thought just then, I thought, "Oh, my, that was fine. I said, "You talk to mother about it. Will you do it?"
He said, "Oh, yes, I'll fix that up; it's all right."
text_no:
Vi giftet oss. Der er du, så hun..? Vi giftet oss. Og rett før vi giftet oss, visste jeg at jeg måtte spørre foreldrene hennes om henne. Hun sa, "Du må spørre mor og far."
Jeg tenkte, "Åh, nei. Nå er det på'n igjen." Jeg sa, "Hvordan med å bare spørre faren din?"
Hun svarte, "Åh, enten den ene eller den andre."
Jeg sa, "Takk, kjære." Jeg gikk ut og husker den kvelden da jeg skulle spørre faren hennes. Jeg satte meg ned, så nervøs. Han lyste opp... Jeg var så nervøs, og hun blikket mot meg og lo. Jeg reiste meg fra sofaen. Jeg gikk bort, og hun sa, "God natt."
Jeg sa, "God natt." Så sa jeg, "Eh, herr Brumbach..."
Han sa, "Ja..."
Jeg sa, "Kan jeg... kan jeg få snakke med deg et øyeblikk?"
Han sa, "Selvfølgelig."
Han kom gående ut, stor kar, og gikk ut på verandaen. Jeg sa, "Det er en fin kveld i kveld."
Han svarte, "Ja, det er det, Billy."
Jeg sa, "Jeg liker dette været, gjør du ikke?" Jeg var så nervøs at jeg ikke visste hva jeg skulle gjøre videre.
Han sa, "Ja." Så sa han, "Fortsett, Bill, du kan få henne."
Jeg spurte, "Mener du det, Charlie?"
Han svarte, "Ja."
Jeg liker ham godt i dag. Jeg tenkte akkurat da, "Åh, dette var flott." Jeg sa, "Kan du snakke med mor om det? Vil du gjøre det?"
Han sa, "Åh, ja, jeg ordner det; det går bra."

text:
I said, "Charlie, look," I said, "I can't make her a living the way you do." I said, "I--I--I'm only making twenty cents an hour." I said, "I--I can't make her… But Charlie, I love her with all my heart, and I'll work as hard as I can to make her a living."
He put his hand on my shoulder, a fine man, said, "Billy, I'd rather you have her than anybody I know of." He said, "You'll be good to her. And after all, happiness don't consist of how much the world's good you own." That's right, friend.
And I said, "Charlie, we'll be happy."
And he said, "Well, I hope that you are, Billy." And he said, "Be good to her."
I said, "I will."
He said, "I'm glad to know that you're going to get married."
text_no:
Jeg sa, "Charlie, se," og fortsatte, "jeg kan ikke forsørge henne slik du gjør." Jeg sa, "Jeg tjener bare tjue cent i timen." Jeg fortsatte, "Jeg kan ikke gi henne det samme... Men Charlie, jeg elsker henne av hele mitt hjerte, og jeg vil jobbe så hardt jeg kan for å forsørge henne."
Han la hånden på skulderen min, en fin mann, og sa, "Billy, jeg vil heller at du har henne enn noen andre jeg kjenner." Han sa, "Du vil være god mot henne. Og tross alt, lykke ligger ikke i hvor mye av verdens goder du eier." Det er sant, venn.
Jeg sa, "Charlie, vi blir lykkelige."
Han sa, "Vel, jeg håper dere blir det, Billy." Og han sa, "Vær god mot henne."
Jeg sa, "Det skal jeg."
Han sa, "Jeg er glad for å vite at du skal gifte deg."

text:
When we were married, we were happy. And oh, my, we didn't have nothing. I remember when we went to housekeeping, we rented two rooms, and I went out and bought an old second hand cooking stove, and I paid a dollar and seventy-five cents for it and paid a dollar for the grates to go in it. Somewhere I went to Sears and Roebucks and got one of them tables, breakfast sets that's not painted. And I remember I painted a big shamrock on it, 'cause I was Irish. I painted a big shamrock on it; she just laughed about it.
We had an old folding bed. Anybody know what a folding bed is? Somebody give it to us and we had two linoleum rugs, and an old folding bed. We went to housekeeping, but brother, it was home. We had each other and that's all we cared for.
text_no:
Da vi giftet oss, var vi lykkelige. Og selv om vi ikke hadde noe særlig, var vi fornøyde. Jeg husker da vi skulle etablere husholdning, leide vi to rom. Jeg kjøpte en gammel brukt komfyr for en dollar og syttifem cent og betalte en dollar for ristene. Et eller annet sted fikk jeg tak i et frokostbordsett fra Sears og Roebuck, som ikke var malt. Jeg husker at jeg malte en stor kløver på det, fordi jeg var irsk. Jeg malte en stor kløver på det, og hun bare lo av det.
Vi hadde en gammel sammenleggbar seng. Noen som vet hva en sammenleggbar seng er? Noen ga den til oss, og vi hadde to linoleumstepper og en gammel sammenleggbar seng. Vi skulle etablere husholdning, men Bror, det var hjem. Vi hadde hverandre, og det var alt vi brydde oss om.

text:
I become a minister and was preaching the Gospel then, and I had a little church and was preaching the Gospel. I didn't make very much. And after awhile God blessed us in our home, and my little Billy Paul, which is setting back there in the audience, come on the scene. I asked God to give me a little boy, and when he was born in the hospital, I first heard him scream in the room, delivery room; I said, "Lord, it's a boy, and I now give him to you. His name shall be called Billy Paul." And the doctor come out in a few minutes, said, "You have a fine boy in there. You like to see him?"
I said, "Yes, his name is Billy Paul." And so he come out and here he is with me today."
text_no:
Jeg ble en forkynner og begynte å preke Evangeliet. Jeg hadde en liten menighet og prekte Evangeliet, men tjente ikke mye. Etter en stund velsignet Gud hjemmet vårt, og min lille Billy Paul, som sitter der i publikum, kom inn i våre liv. Jeg ba Gud om å gi meg en liten gutt, og da han ble født på sykehuset, hørte jeg hans første skrik i fødestuen. Jeg sa: "Herre, det er en gutt, og jeg gir ham nå til Deg. Hans navn skal være Billy Paul." Legen kom ut noen minutter senere og sa: "Du har fått en flott gutt. Vil du se ham?" 
Jeg sa: "Ja, hans navn er Billy Paul." Og her er han sammen med meg i dag.

text:
Went on and we struggled and worked and tried to make a living and go on the best we could. I'll hurry right through now as quick as possible to this tear strained part.
And then, why we're doing fine. We saved our money. I was still paying for my old Ford. And I--I remember, we got a time where we was--I could go up to Dowagiac, Michigan. Old Brother Ryan, he's somewhere in here today. I believe you'll see him along here. He's coming in. He's an old man. I used to thought he belonged to the House of David, 'cause he had a long beard and hair. I went to see him at Michigan to fish a little while. We'd saved our money, and she wanted to stay home 'cause they had a--a working group in the church, and she couldn't leave to go with me. And I could drive. It was about two hundred or better miles up there. I went up and spent two or three days fishing.
text_no:
Vi fortsatte å kjempe, arbeide og forsøke å tjene til livets opphold så godt vi kunne. Jeg skal skynde meg gjennom denne følelsesladde delen.
Vi hadde det bra. Vi sparte penger. Jeg betalte fortsatt på min gamle Ford. Jeg husker at det kom en tid da jeg kunne dra til Dowagiac, Michigan. Gamle Bror Ryan, han er her et sted i dag. Jeg tror dere vil se ham her. Han kommer inn. Han er en eldre mann. Jeg pleide å tro at han tilhørte Davids Hus, fordi han hadde langt skjegg og hår. Jeg dro for å besøke ham i Michigan for å fiske litt. Vi hadde spart penger, og hun ønsket å bli hjemme fordi de hadde en arbeidsgruppe i menigheten, og hun kunne ikke dra sammen med meg. Jeg kunne kjøre. Det var rundt to hundre eller flere mil dit. Jeg dro opp og tilbrakte to eller tre dager med å fiske.

text:
And on my road back, I passed through Mishawaka, Indiana. And there was a people out there, and the worst church manners I ever seen in my life: they were screaming and…?… Well, I never seen anything act like that. So I thought, "I believe I'll see." And it was a Pentecostal group of people. The minister was named Raugh, where they was holding… Might somebody might know Reverend Mr. Raugh from Mishawaka, Indiana.
And they had a Pentecostal group. Well, I went and stopped and went to the door and police was directing the traffic on the outside. They was all up and down through there, and those people were clapping their hands, and screaming, and running up and down the aisles and shouting. I thought, "My, my, what a group." Why have you heard that little song, "Something got a hold of me"? It beginning to work on me a little bit, you know. Well, I thought, "Well…"
text_no:
På vei tilbake kjørte jeg gjennom Mishawaka, Indiana. Der støtte jeg på en gruppe mennesker med de verste kirkevanene jeg noensinne har sett: de skrek og oppførte seg på en måte jeg aldri har sett før. Jeg tenkte: "Jeg tror jeg vil se nærmere på dette." Det viste seg å være en pinsegruppe. Forkynneren het Raugh. Kanskje noen kjenner til Pastor Raugh fra Mishawaka, Indiana.
Denne pinsegruppen holdt et møte der, og jeg gikk bort til døren. Politiet dirigerte trafikken utenfor, og folk sto langs hele gaten. Inne i lokalet klappet de, skrek, løp opp og ned i midtgangen og ropte. Jeg tenkte: "Herre min, for en gjeng." Har dere hørt den lille sangen, "Something Got a Hold of Me"? Den begynte å påvirke meg litt. Jeg tenkte: "Vel..."

text: I counted my money. I had exactly two dollars and seventy-five cents and had to go home. So I counted how much gasoline it would take me: a tank of gas to get home. I had seventy-five cents left. So they… Going on, their meeting was going on, they was having a conference, a national conference. And I went down and got me a whole big bunch of stale rolls with sugar on them and wrapped them up and put them under my seat. And I knowed I could live off of that. Although they was having dinner there, and so forth, but I had no money to put in, so I didn't want to eat from them when I couldn't put nothing in the offering. So I couldn't find a place to stay, so I went out in the country in an old corn field and stretched out my seat out of my car, and put my trousers under there to press them that night, and laid down.
text_no: Jeg telte pengene mine. Jeg hadde nøyaktig to dollar og syttifem cent og måtte dra hjem. Jeg regnet ut hvor mye bensin jeg trengte: en full tank for å komme hjem og hadde syttifem cent igjen. Møtet deres, som var en nasjonal konferanse, fortsatte. Jeg gikk og kjøpte en stor haug med gamle boller med sukker på, pakket dem sammen og la dem under setet. Jeg visste at jeg kunne leve på det. Selv om de hadde middag der og så videre, hadde jeg ingen penger til å bidra med, og jeg ville ikke spise uten å kunne gi noe i kollekten. Jeg fant ingen plass å overnatte, så jeg dro ut på landet til en gammel kornåker, strakk setet i bilen og la buksene mine under setet for å presse dem om natten, og la meg ned.

text:
And I know that night they had a big bunch there, and they begin to preach, and they said, "All the preachers come to the platform." Two or three hundred preachers walked to the platform. And they had different ones, they said, "Now we haven't got time for all of you to preach. Just stand up and give your name, and where you are. And when it come my time, I said, "Reverend Branham, Jeffersonville, Indiana." Set down. "Evangelist," like that. Just on next like that and went on through.
And directly he said some man was going to preach, a colored man. They had to have it in the north, 'cause they couldn't have it in the south on account of the colored and white together. They brought an old fellow out there; he had on one of those great big old preacher coats, you know, them long… Old colored man, a little bit of a rim of hair around like that come walking out like this, you know. And I'd heard all good preaching that day. He walked up.
text_no:
Den kvelden var det en stor forsamling, og de begynte å preke. De sa: "Alle forkynnere, kom opp på plattformen." To eller tre hundre forkynnere gikk opp på plattformen. De sa: "Vi har ikke tid til at alle skal preke. Bare reis dere opp, si navn og hvor dere kommer fra." Da det ble min tur, sa jeg: "Reverend Branham, Jeffersonville, Indiana." Satt meg ned. "Evangelist," la jeg til. Og så gikk det videre.
Deretter kunngjorde de at en mann skulle preke, en farget mann. De måtte ha det i nord fordi de ikke kunne ha det i sør på grunn av segregeringen. En gammel mann ble ført fram; han hadde på seg en stor, gammel predikantfrakk. En gammel farget mann med en liten hårkant gikk rolig fram. Jeg hadde allerede hørt mye god forkynnelse den dagen. Han gikk opp til faltformen.

text:
First time I ever seen a microphone. And I was watching the public addressing outfit, little bitty thing hanging there at the big tabernacle. My, it all looked good to me. And I was looking around, you know, but what amazed me is those people so free and happy. My, I didn't--was used to that.
And then this old man come out there. I thought, "Look like they'd have some of the young men preach instead of that old man, about dead. And he come walking out there like this; he said, "Well," he said, "Children…" He got to talking, you know. He took his text.
Now preachers been preaching about the baptism of the Holy Spirit, and I was listening close, and all these things. But he took his text from back over in Job: "Where was you when I laid the foundations of the world, when the morning stars sang together and sons of God shouted for joy?" and he begin to preach on that and him… They preach those things down here, but he went up, back there with Job, plumb down through eternity, and down, and come back on the horizontal rainbow coming. About time he got preaching there about five minutes, that old stoved up man let out a yelp, and screamed and jumped up in the air, and clicked his heels together, throwed his chest back, tipped off that platform like a little bantam chicken, said, "Well, you ain't even got room up here for me to preach."
I looked at that, and I said, "My, that's what I want. If it'll make an old man act like that, what would it do to me?" If it's that…?… Why, I thought, "That's what I want." And I said, "I ain't going home till I find out more about this."
text_no:
Dette var første gang jeg så en mikrofon. Jeg la merke til det lille apparatet som hang der i den store tabernaklet. Alt virket så imponerende for meg. Det som virkelig rørte meg, var hvor frie og lykkelige folk var. Det var jeg ikke vant til.
Så kom en eldre mann ut på scenen. Jeg tenkte, "De kunne vel latt en av de unge mennene tale i stedet for denne gamle mannen, han ser jo nesten død ut." Han kom gående ut og sa, "Vel," og fortsatte, "Barn…" Så begynte han å tale og tok for seg sitt skriftsted.
Forkynnere hadde snakket om dåpen i Den Hellige Ånd, og jeg lyttet nøye. Men han tok sitt skriftsted fra Job: "Hvor var du da Jeg la jordens grunnvoller, da morgenstjernene sang sammen og Guds sønner jublet av glede?" Så begynte han å preke om det, fra evigheten og tilbake til regnbuen som strakte seg horisontalt. Etter bare fem minutter av talen, ropte den gamle mannen ut, hoppet opp i luften, klasket hælene sammen, strakte brystet frem og sa, "Vel, det er knapt nok plass her oppe for meg å preke."
Jeg så på det og tenkte, "Det er det jeg vil ha. Hvis det kan få en gammel mann til å oppføre seg slik, hva ville det da gjøre med meg?" Jeg bestemte meg, "Jeg drar ikke hjem før jeg finner ut mer om dette."

text:
That night, out there I knelt down. I said, "Oh, God, them people's got something that I want." I said, "Let me have some of that." And I was… Listen, I said, "Now, give me favor with them somewhere. When I go down there," I said, "May I'll just… Maybe just them things get all over me and I'll feel that way."
So I went on down there. You know the Baptists don't act like that, you know, they have the… You know. And now, no disregards to the Baptist church, now, and that's all right. If the Baptists would've stayed back…
text_no:
Denne kvelden knelte jeg ned der ute og sa: "Å, Gud, disse menneskene har noe som jeg vil ha." Jeg sa: "La meg få noe av det." Og jeg var... Hør her, jeg sa: "Nå, gi meg velvilje hos dem. Når jeg drar dit," sa jeg, "måtte disse tingene bare omgi meg, så jeg kan føle det samme."
Så dro jeg dit. Du vet, baptistene oppfører seg ikke slik. Du vet hvordan de er. Og nå mener jeg ikke noe negativt om baptistmenigheten; den er helt i orden. Hvis baptistene hadde holdt seg tilbake...

text:
Listen, not long ago I was preaching in a Baptist church just as hard as I could preach, and all of them setting there just as starchy. I said, "Say, is this the Baptist church?"
Pastor said, "Yes, sir."
I got up preaching again, and I was getting a little wild, I guess you know that. So I got a…?… preaching up like that, nobody said, "Amen." I said, "Say, is this the Baptist church?"
Said, "Yes, sir."
I said, "Look, you all not Baptist; you're just church joiners. Down in Kentucky where I come from when we Baptists down there, when we get saved, we'd be down at the altar and beat one another on the back till we come through. That's real Baptists, brother." That's right. That's the kind of Baptists we need today. That's right. That's exactly right.
text_no:
Hør, for litt siden preket jeg i en baptistmenighet så hardt jeg kunne, og alle satt der stive som pinner. Jeg sa: "Si meg, er dette en baptistmenighet?"
Pastoren svarte: "Ja, sir."
Jeg begynte å forkynne igjen og ble litt ivrig, dere vet hvordan det er. Så jeg fortsatte å forkynne som før, men ingen sa "Amen." Jeg spurte igjen: "Er dette en baptistmenighet?"
Han svarte: "Ja, sir."
Da sa jeg: "Hør her, dere er ikke ekte baptister; dere er bare kirkemedlemmer. I Kentucky, hvor jeg kommer fra, når vi baptister blir frelst, ligger vi ved alteret og slår hverandre på ryggen til vi kommer igjennom. Det er ekte baptister, Bror." Det er riktig. Det er den typen baptister vi trenger i dag. Det er helt riktig.

text:
And then I… But these people… I remember going on and praising the Lord. And I went out and I prayed, "God, let me get some of that. That's what I want."
Next day, I went out to church about ten o'clock. They just got through breakfast, and I'd had my rolls. And I come down and got me a drink at a fountain down there in the city, drove my old Ford up, got out. I put on a pair of them seersucker trousers that morning, and a little old tee shirt. "Nobody knowed me, not a soul." I walked in, set down by a colored man. And I was setting there, you know, and I thought, "Oh, my. " All of them was clapping their hands and singing and shouting and screaming. I thought, "Oh, my, if I just had grace enough to do that. If I can just get loose to do that, I want that."
And I was watching. And bye and bye after awhile the little fellow come up to the microphone. His name was Kirch, from Cincinnati, and--Ohio. And he said, "There was a young minister on the platform last night by the name of Branham." Said, "We want him this morning to come to the platform for the morning message." Seersucker trousers, tee shirt… My… Not me.
text_no:
Og så… Men disse menneskene… Jeg husker at jeg fortsatte å prise Herren. Jeg ba: "Gud, la meg få litt av det. Det er det jeg vil ha."
Neste dag dro jeg til menigheten rundt klokken ti. De hadde akkurat spist frokost, og jeg hadde fått mine rundstykker. Jeg gikk ned og tok en drink ved en fontene i byen, kjørte opp med min gamle Ford, steg ut. Jeg hadde på meg et par seersucker-bukser og en gammel t-skjorte. "Ingen kjente meg, ikke en sjel." Jeg satte meg ved siden av en farget mann. Der satt jeg og tenkte: "Å, herlighet." Alle klappet, sang, ropte og skrek. Jeg tenkte: "Å, hvis jeg bare hadde nok nåde til å gjøre det. Hvis jeg bare kunne slippe meg løs og gjøre det, det er det jeg vil."
Jeg observerte. Etter en stund kom en liten kar opp til mikrofonen. Hans navn var Kirch, fra Cincinnati, Ohio. Han sa: "Det var en ung forkynner på plattformen i går kveld ved navn Branham. Vi vil at han skal komme til plattformen for morgenens budskap." Seersucker-bukser, t-skjorte… Nei, ikke jeg.

text:
I was way back there; I just kind scooted down in the seat like this. So they begin and he went in and he sang another song. He said, "Anybody inside or out know the whereabouts of Mr. Branham?" I was the youngest preacher on the platform. He said, "Come to the platform," said, "we--you're to bring the morning message." I just set real close, never said nothing.
That colored fellow looked over at me said, "Do you know him?"
Oh, my. What could I say? I said, "Yes, sir."
He said, "Go get him."
Oh, I--I--I couldn't lie to him. You see? I said, "Listen, fellow, I want to tell you something."
Said, "Yes, sir."
I said, "I--I--I'm--I'm Brother Branham, you see," but I said, "I can't get up there." I said, "Look at these trousers. See? Look at this tee shirt."
He said, "Them people don't care how you dress."
I said, "But look, I--I--I can't go up there." I said, "You just keep still. Don't tell them I'm here. (See?) Just keep still."
He said, "Anybody know where Reverend Branham is?"
That colored man said, "Here he is. Here he is." Seersucker trousers, tee shirt, my… My face was red, you know. Oh, my. What was I going to do? I got ahold of my Bible. I said, "Lord, I prayed last night for something, maybe it's You." I said, "I don't know what I'm going to do. I can't preach around where them fellows are." I'll go up there. And I started to walking up there. Everybody was looking at me, you know, and them seersucker trousers. And you know how they'd be pressed under a Ford seat, so… This--this old tee shirt, and it stained all over, you know.
text_no:
Jeg satt langt bak og hadde sunket litt ned i setet. De begynte, og han sang en annen sang. Så sa han: "Er det noen her eller utenfor som vet hvor Herr Branham er?" Jeg var den yngste forkynneren på plattformen. Han sa: "Kom til plattformen. Du skal gi morgenens budskap." Jeg satt bare stille og sa ingenting.
En farget mann så på meg og spurte: "Kjenner du ham?"
Å, hva kunne jeg si? Jeg sa: "Ja, sir."
Han sa: "Gå og hent ham."
Jeg kunne ikke lyve for ham, skjønner du. Jeg sa: "Hør her, jeg vil fortelle deg noe."
Han sa: "Ja, sir."
Jeg svarte: "Jeg er Bror Branham, ser du, men jeg kan ikke gå opp der. Se på disse buksene. Se på denne t-skjorten."
Han sa: "Folk bryr seg ikke om hvordan du er kledd."
Jeg sa: "Men se, jeg kan ikke gå opp der. Hold det hemmelig, ikke si noe om at jeg er her. Bare hold det hemmelig."
Han ropte: "Er det noen som vet hvor Pastor Branham er?"
Den fargede mannen sa: "Her er han. Her er han." Jeg hadde seersucker-bukser og t-skjorte på meg, og ansiktet mitt ble rødt. Å, hva skulle jeg gjøre? Jeg fikk tak i Bibelen min og sa: "Herre, jeg ba i går kveld om noe, kanskje det er Deg." Jeg visste ikke hva jeg skulle gjøre. Jeg kunne ikke forkynne foran disse mennene. Men jeg gikk opp, og alle så på meg, med de seersucker-buksene som var presset under et Ford-sete, og den gamle t-skjorten som var flekkete over det hele.

text: I started walking up…?… I was up fishing. So I--I started walking up there and I said, "Why, why, people," I said, "I'm just a little bit… I don't know what I am," I said. "I--I--I feel kindly funny," I said, "I--I--I'm not used to your all's religion," I said. "And…" And I said, "I--I--I wanted to speak just a little; I'll do the best I can." And so I got up over there--over there where the rich man lifted up his eyes in hell, and my, something got ahold of me. The next thing I knowed it was about a half-hour later. I was out in yard just. My, what a time we had.
text_no: Jeg begynte å gå opp... jeg var oppe og fisket. Da jeg begynte å gå opp der og sa: "Hvorfor, hvorfor, folkens," sa jeg, "jeg er bare litt... jeg vet ikke hva jeg er," sa jeg. "Jeg føler meg litt rar," sa jeg, "jeg er ikke vant til deres religion," sa jeg. "Og..." Så sa jeg, "Jeg ville bare si litt; jeg skal gjøre mitt beste." Da jeg kom dit hvor den rike mannen løftet opp øynene i helvete, grep noe fatt i meg. Det neste jeg visste, var at det hadde gått omtrent en halv time. Jeg var ute i gården. For en tid vi hadde.

text:
Here come a fellow up with a big pair of Texas boots on, a big hat, said, "I'm Reverend so-and-so." I thought, "Well, I ain't so bad dressed after all." I said, "Are you a minister?"
He said, "Sure." Said, "I heard your message." Said, "I heard you was a evangelist." Like for you to come down to Texas and hold me a revival." I said, "Well, look, I'm just a young preacher. I just started." I said, "I ain't--hadn't got into this way just right, with you all. Oh," I said, "I like it."
text_no:
En mann kom bort med et par store Texas-støvler og en stor hatt og sa: "Jeg er pastor så-og-så." Jeg tenkte: "Vel, jeg er ikke så dårlig kledd likevel." Jeg spurte: "Er du en forkynner?"
Han svarte: "Selvfølgelig. Jeg har hørt budskapet ditt. Jeg hørte at du var en evangelist. Jeg vil gjerne at du kommer til Texas og holder en vekkelse for meg." Jeg sa: "Vel, se, jeg er bare en ung predikant. Jeg har akkurat begynt." Jeg fortsatte: "Jeg har ikke kommet ordentlig i gang på denne måten, sammen med dere. Åh," la jeg til, "men jeg liker det."

text:
A fellow tapped me on the shoulder. And he looked and he had on a pair of those there knickerbocker pants, bloused at the knees, like I used to wear when I was a little boy: golf clothes. He said, "I'm Elder so-and-so from Miami."
I said, "You a preacher?"
He said, "Yes, sir."
I thought, "Well, I'm all right then."
So he said, "Why, here." Said, "Will you come and hold a meeting for me?"
text_no:
En mann klappet meg på skulderen. Han hadde på seg et par knebukser som jeg pleide å bruke som liten gutt, golfklær. Han sa: "Jeg er Eldste så-og-så fra Miami."
Jeg spurte: "Er du en forkynner?"
"Ja, sir," svarte han.
Jeg tenkte: "Da er jeg i orden."
Han fortsatte: "Vil du komme og holde et møte for meg?"

text:
And a lady come up from some, oh, way up in the Indian country, said, "Oh brother, we need you up there."
I thought, "Thank You, Lord. Thank You. Maybe this is what You're going to do something for me." I got on over there; and I begin to take down their names and address. I said, "I'll find out. I'll talk it over with the Lord, with my family…"
When I went home, I'll never forget. Running in… My wife always met me with her arms stretched out; I can see her yet today, bless her heart. She come running to the door; I said, "Oh, honey, I got something to tell you. I met the best bunch of people in the world. I begin to tell her all about how they was acting. She said, "Well, where they at?"
I said, "Up at Mishawaka, Indiana." I said, "You think they're ashamed of their religion? They just scream and shout, run, shake one anther's hands." I said, "They don't care. They're just as free like birds in the air." I said…
text_no:
En dame fra et sted langt oppe i det indiske området kom og sa: "Åh, bror, vi trenger deg der oppe."
Jeg tenkte: "Takk, Herre. Takk. Kanskje dette er noe Du vil gjøre for meg." Jeg dro dit og begynte å notere navn og adresser. Jeg sa: "Jeg skal finne ut av det. Jeg skal snakke med Herren og med familien min…"
Da jeg kom hjem, vil jeg aldri glemme det. Jeg løp inn... Min kone møtte meg alltid med utstrakte armer; jeg kan fortsatt se henne for meg i dag, velsigne hennes hjerte. Hun kom løpende til døren, og jeg sa: "Åh, kjære, jeg har noe å fortelle deg. Jeg har møtt den beste gruppen mennesker i verden." Jeg begynte å fortelle henne om hvordan de oppførte seg. Hun sa: "Hvor er de?"
Jeg sa: "I Mishawaka, Indiana." Jeg sa: "Tror du de skammer seg over sin religion? De bare skriker og roper, hilser på hverandre." Jeg sa: "De bryr seg ikke. De er like frie som fugler i lufta."

text:
She said, "Were they at?"
And I said, "Up in Mishawaka. A whole bunch of them up there." And I want to show you something. Reached down in my pocket. I said, "They have given me invitations to come to their churches to preach." I said, "You believe it?"
She said, "Is that true, honey?"
I say, "Yeah."
She said, "Well, maybe you couldn't preach in their kind of church."
I said, "That's what they asked me to do." I said, "Well, I'll go." So she… Well, I said, "Would you go with me?" God bless her heart, she'd always go.
She said, "Yes, honey, wherever you go I'll go with you." We didn't have no money. We went in and counted our money, what we had, and I didn't have even enough to make the payment on the car. I said, "Well look, honey, the--the--the Bible said don't take nothing and take no thought." I said, "The Bible said if you got two coats, give one of them to your brother and go on out and He'd supply." Now, I said, "Do you think if you and the baby could go on and be…"
"Yes, yes, we'll do it."
text_no:
Hun spurte, "Hvor var de?"
Jeg svarte, "Oppe i Mishawaka. En hel gjeng av dem der oppe." Jeg vil vise deg noe. Jeg rakte ned i lommen og sa, "De har gitt meg invitasjoner til å komme til menighetene deres for å forkynne." Jeg spurte, "Tror du det?"
Hun sa, "Er det sant, kjære?"
Jeg svarte, "Ja."
Hun sa, "Kanskje du ikke kunne forkynne i deres type menighet."
Jeg sa, "Det er det de spurte meg om å gjøre." Jeg sa, "Vel, jeg skal dra." Så hun… Jeg sa, "Vil du bli med meg?" Gud velsigne hennes hjerte, hun ville alltid bli med.
Hun sa, "Ja, kjære, hvor enn du går, vil jeg gå med deg." Vi hadde ikke penger. Vi gikk inn og telte hva vi hadde, og det var ikke engang nok til å betale avdraget på bilen. Jeg sa, "Se her, kjære, Bibelen sier ikke å ta med noe og ikke tenke på det." Jeg sa, "Bibelen sier at hvis du har to kjortler, gi en til din bror og dra ut; Han vil sørge for deg." Så spurte jeg, "Tror du at du og babyen kan klare dere?"
"Ja, ja, vi gjør det," sa hun.

text:
Next thing we had to tell our parents then. And I went and told my mama here. Of course mama, it's all right with her. I'm thankful for a good mother and said, "Sure, honey, God bless you."
Then we had to go. And this time her father and mother had separated, so I had to go to her mother. And when I went to her mother, she said, "William…"
Oh, my. I knew what was up. She said, "You cannot take my daughter."
I said, "Look, a… Look, them's the nicest bunch of people."
She said, "I've heard of that bunch of people; they're holy rollers." Then she said, "You're not pulling my girl out across this world. Today she's got something to eat and tomorrow she's starving amongst that bunch of trash." And brother, I come to find out this, and I say it from my heart. "What she called trash is the cream of the crop." That's exactly right. I say that with reverence. That's true. Now.
text_no:
Neste steg var å fortelle foreldrene våre. Jeg gikk og fortalte det til mamma først. Selvfølgelig var det greit for henne. Jeg er takknemlig for en god mor som sa: "Selvfølgelig, kjære, Gud velsigne deg."
Så måtte vi fortelle hennes mor, ettersom hennes foreldre var separert. Da jeg gikk til hennes mor, sa hun: "William…"
Å, jeg visste hva som kom. Hun sa: "Du kan ikke ta datteren min."
Jeg svarte: "Se, det er den beste gruppen mennesker."
Hun sa: "Jeg har hørt om den gruppen; de er hellige rullere." Så sa hun: "Du skal ikke dra min datter rundt i verden. I dag har hun noe å spise, og i morgen sulter hun blant den gjengen med søppel."
Og, bror, jeg fant senere ut noe, og jeg sier det fra hjertet. "Det hun kalte søppel, er kremmen av samfunnet." Det er helt riktig. Jeg sier det med ærbødighet. Det er sant.

text:
She said… And I said, "Well, she's my wife and she wants you…"
She said, "Well, mama, I want…" Well, there it was.
And I said… She started… She said, "Well, if she goes, her mother will go to the grave broken hearted." And the wife started crying.
Well now, I couldn't stand that, so I told her; I said, "Well, we'll wait and go later." Now, there's where I made my mistake." Now, if I would have went on, this gift would have been in operation way before that, 'cause I'd come amongst people who would recognize it. You see? But I said, "Well, we won't go."
And brother, sister, from right then my trouble started. The first thing, you know, my church begin to drop off. My brother was killed, having his neck broke in the street. He was riding in a car like this with his arm out; his neck was broke; his blood poured out on my other brother's body. And I run to meet him, but it was too far, died before I could get to him. My sister-in-law died a few days after that. My father died in my arms. Everything begin to go wrong.
Then the '37 Flood came on. You heard about it, many of you on your radios and things. The Ohio River, spread out through the country.
text_no:
Hun sa, og jeg svarte, "Vel, hun er min kone og hun ønsker deg..." 
Hun sa, "Vel, mamma, jeg vil..." Der var det.
Hun begynte å si noe, og jeg svarte... Hun sa: "Vel, om hun drar, vil moren hennes dø av sorg." Og kona mi begynte å gråte.
Da klarte jeg ikke å stå imot, så jeg sa, "Vel, vi venter og drar senere." Der gjorde jeg min feil. Hadde jeg dratt med en gang, ville denne gaven vært i virke mye tidligere, fordi jeg hadde vært blant folk som anerkjente den. Men jeg sa, "Vel, vi drar ikke."
Brødre og søstre, det var da problemene mine begynte. Først begynte menigheten min å falle fra. Min bror ble drept, han brakk nakken i en ulykke. Han satt i bilen med armen ut, og nakken ble brukket; blodet hans rant ut over kroppen til en annen bror. Jeg løp for å møte ham, men det var for sent; han døde før jeg rakk frem. Min svigerinne døde noen få dager etterpå, og min far døde i armene mine. Alt begynte å gå galt.
Så kom flommen i '37. Mange av dere hørte om den på radioen. Ohio-elven spredte seg gjennom hele landet.

text:
My wife taken pneumonia. Little old Dr. Adair, I shall never forget him. He come. We're buddies. We'd fish together, and hunt together, and everything: one of the best doctors, medical doctors there is in the country. And he… We went to school together. He come up there and looked at her, said, "Billy," said, "that girl's got pneumonia." I'd just taken him his Christmas present. He--he never…
God had give us a little girl between that time, little Sharon Rose. I couldn't call her the Rose of Sharon, but I could call her Sharon Rose. And I called her Sharon Rose. And God gave her to us and she was a sweet little lump, and we just loved her so much. And she'd just got to a place to where, the mother would set her in her little, the "four corners," you know, out in the yard, and I'd come up, and I'd blow the horn on the car like that. She'd recognize and lift her little arms and go, "Goo, goo, goo, goo." My, how I loved that little lump of human flesh. I'd hold her in my bosom and kiss her and love her. My little boy…
I just love little children, and God had give them to me. And I'd put them both on my back and piggyback and ride around, you know, just as happy as we could be, nothing…?… but just… She had just the two children in a little over two years. And then…
text_no:
Min kone fikk lungebetennelse. Den lille gamle Dr. Adair, jeg skal aldri glemme ham. Han kom. Vi var kompiser, fisket sammen, jaktet sammen, og alt mulig. Han er en av de beste legene i landet. Vi gikk på skole sammen. Han kom inn og så på henne, sa: "Billy, den jenta har lungebetennelse." Jeg hadde akkurat gitt ham julegaven sin. Han...
Gud hadde gitt oss en liten jente på den tiden, lille Sharon Rose. Jeg kunne ikke kalle henne Rosen fra Sharon, men jeg kunne kalle henne Sharon Rose. Så jeg kalte henne Sharon Rose. Gud ga henne til oss, og hun var en søt liten engel, og vi elsket henne så mye. Hun hadde nettopp kommet til et punkt hvor moren kunne sette henne i "de fire hjørner," du vet, ute i hagen. Når jeg kjørte hjem og tutet med bilhornet, løftet hun de små armene sine og sa "Goo, goo, goo, goo." Åh, hvor jeg elsket den lille klumpen av menneskeliv. Jeg holdt henne i armene, kysset henne og elsket henne. Min lille gutt...
Jeg elsker virkelig små barn, og Gud hadde gitt dem til meg. Jeg plasserte dem begge på ryggen min, lekte "bånnkjør" og gikk rundt, så lykkelige som vi kunne være. Hun hadde bare fått to barn på litt over to år. Og så...

text:
But she taken pneumonia when she went to get the children a Christmas present. And the doctor said she'll have to lay right here, Billy, 'cause she's--probably will die if--if she ever moves. But her mother come up and said she was going to move her down to her house. And Dr. Adair said, "She'll have to get another doctor, 'cause I wouldn't do it, Billy," wouldn't permit it.
So they--she went and dismissed him and got another doctor and taken her down there. And the flood broke through, and then all of us was put on the rescue to--to work with the flood. We rushed her out to the government hospital where they temporary placed the hospital. The dikes was breaking through; the city was washing away.
text_no:
Hun fikk lungebetennelse da hun gikk for å kjøpe julegave til barna. Legen sa: "Hun må bli liggende her, Billy, for hun vil sannsynligvis dø hvis hun beveger seg." Men moren kom opp og sa at hun skulle flytte henne hjem til seg. Dr. Adair svarte: "Da må hun finne en annen lege, for jeg vil ikke tillate det, Billy." 
De avskjediget ham og hentet en ny lege som flyttet henne dit. Så brøt flommen ut, og alle ble satt til redningsarbeid. Vi flyttet henne raskt til et midlertidig oppsatt sykehus i regjeringens regi. Dammene brast, og byen ble skylt bort.

text:
I'll never forget those nights. I remember they called me. Both babies was sick with pneumonia, and she was laying sick with pneumonia, out in the hospital there with a fever a hundred and five and both babies sick.
And I come up… I come up and had my car, and I had a boat and I was raised on the river and said… I operated a boat pretty well. And everybody was just pulling people out of the flood, and people drowning and everything. I got my boat… I was working on a wall and some of them come, said, "Oh, the--get down here, preacher, right quick and get your boat in down here." The dike broke, you know, over on Chestnut Street, and the houses are washed away, and there's a mother on top of the house out there with a bunch of little children, about eleven o'clock at night.
text_no:
Jeg vil aldri glemme de nettene. Jeg husker de ringte meg. Begge barna var syke med lungebetennelse, og hun lå også syk med lungebetennelse på sykehuset med en feber på hundre og fem, og begge barna var syke.
Jeg kom opp... Jeg kom opp og hadde bilen min, og jeg hadde en båt. Jeg var oppvokst ved elva og kunne håndtere en båt ganske godt. Alle dro folk ut av flommen, folk druknet og det var kaos. Jeg fikk båten min... Jeg jobbet på en murvegg, og noen kom og sa: "Åh, kom deg hit raskt, forkynner, og få båten din ut her." Diket ved Chestnut Street hadde bristet, husene ble vasket bort, og det var en mor på toppen av et hus med en gjeng små barn, rundt klokken elleve om natten.

text: I rushed down there real quick and throwed my boat in the water, started it up. And I had to buck those waves as high as this tent, almost, up in there where they just a dashing against the side of those buildings like that. And I heard a mother screaming. I looked over there, and she was standing on the top porch, out over her house, and the--the waves just shaking that house like that with four or five little children standing around her. I said, "See where the street lights hadn't went out yet, down through there." And I went up through the alley like this with the boat and it just washing things from under it.
